title: Sovereign debt markets: post-pandemic developments and challenges
summary: Bank of Italy Deputy Governor Paolo Angelini delivered a welcome address at the 11th International Conference on Sovereign Bond Markets reviewing four post-pandemic developments shaping sovereign debt markets: rising global public debt (~90% of GDP, projected to reach ~100% by 2030), debt distress affecting roughly half of low-income countries, normalization of interest rates to levels materially above pre-pandemic lows, and ongoing quantitative tightening by major central banks since 2022.
key_points: Global gross public debt rose to approximately 90% of GDP in 2025, up from 84% in 2019, with projections indicating nearly 100% by 2030
The US and China are expected to experience the sharpest rises in public debt among major economies, raising sustainability concerns given the dollar's global reserve status
Approximately half of low-income countries are facing or approaching debt distress; their external debt reached nearly $1 trillion at end-2024, up one-third since the pandemic
Interest rates have returned to 'normal' levels in most countries and are not expected to revert to the low levels of the pre-pandemic decade
The combination of higher debt levels and higher interest rates is expected to increase debt servicing costs materially
QT-related net supply of government bonds has been absorbed predominantly by non-banks (investment funds, foreign investors, households) with significant cross-country heterogeneity
EU fiscal reforms have constrained euro area debt-to-GDP growth projections to modest increases through 2030
why_it_matters: Sovereign bond markets are foundational to global finance—they transmit monetary policy, establish benchmarks for pricing other assets, and serve as primary safe collateral. The convergence of elevated debt levels, structurally higher rates, and ongoing central bank balance sheet reduction creates compounded challenges for debt management, financial stability, and monetary policy transmission. The US debt trajectory is flagged as particularly consequential given dollar dominance and potential spillovers to emerging markets.
macro_context: Rising global debt-to-GDP ratios (~90% in 2025 to ~100% by 2030) combined with structurally higher interest rates create an inflationary bias and constrain monetary policy flexibility. The US fiscal sustainability concerns carry particular weight due to the dollar's reserve currency role and potential for sovereign yield spikes to trigger risk-off episodes. Debt distress in low-income countries (2% of global GDP but 20% of world population) presents humanitarian and geopolitical risks rather than systemic financial stability risks at present.
market_structure_context: The speech discusses how sovereign bonds function as safe assets and collateral, their role in regulatory capital and liquidity frameworks, and how different investor classes (banks, non-banks, foreign investors) have absorbed QT-related supply shifts. The market structure implications include: (1) sovereign bonds remain systemically important as benchmarks and collateral; (2) QT is reshaping the investor base with non-banks playing a larger role; (3) heterogeneity across countries (e.g., Italian households' increased share vs. banks' declining share) reflects structural differences in domestic financial systems.
important_numbers: 90% of GDP (global gross public debt in 2025)
84% of GDP (global gross public debt in 2019)
100% of GDP (projected global gross public debt by 2030)
62% of GDP (global debt before Global Financial Crisis)
~$1 trillion (external debt of low-income countries at end-2024)
1/3 increase (low-income country external debt growth since pandemic)

notes: Historical record (published June 11, 2025). Speaker: Paolo Angelini, Deputy Governor of Bank of Italy. Conference joint endeavor among Bank of Italy, Deutsche Bundesbank, Bank of Canada, ECB, and academic institutions. The speech opens with historical context on Venetian sovereign debt innovation (prestiti, late 13th century). Source truncated; key sections on QT challenges and debt manager perspectives may be omitted. Market impact confidence lowered because speech is backward-looking synthesis rather than forward policy guidance.
